title: 田纳西河谷管理局因违反核法规、隐瞒事实被罚款90万美元
title_en: Tennessee Valley Authority fined $900K for nuclear violations, coverup

note:
上周二，马萨诸塞州的一项维修权法案以压倒性胜利获得通过。这项措施在投票中被称为“问题1”，它将迫使汽车制造商确保独立的维修店能够获得与制造商经营的维修店相同的诊断工具。这将改变马萨诸塞州的汽车维修格局，特别是对特斯拉(Tesla)来说。特斯拉是一家臭名昭著的神秘公司，它让电动汽车的维修变得困难而昂贵。
里奇·伯努伊特是一名YouTuber和机械师，专门负责修复受损的特斯拉汽车。他表示，这项维修权法案改变了游戏规则，但仍有很多工作要做。Benoit在2018年的一部纪录片中向Motherboard详细介绍了特斯拉软件的某些方面，他在采购零部件和访问特斯拉软件的某些方面多次遇到问题。
Benoit在电话中告诉Motherboard：“对我们来说，这意味着从2022年车型年开始，制造商必须向许多第三方提供他们在经销商处使用的相同工具。”
Benoit很兴奋，但他知道获取信息和诊断工具将是一场战斗。他之所以知道这一点，是因为特斯拉以前曾反对过这类法律。2012年通过了一项类似的法律，规定经销商必须向维修商店提供服务手册和诊断信息。值得注意的是，特斯拉也不是全国汽车制造商签署的谅解备忘录的一部分，该备忘录实际上使马萨诸塞州的立法成为一项全国性法律。
“他们通过说…来绕过这个问题。他们没有经销商。“Benoit说。“因此，从技术上讲，他们不需要做任何事情。”
但特斯拉确实提供了一些信息。伯努瓦说：“出于他们的好心，他们做了一个门户网站，这样你就可以登录并访问他们的诊断信息。”“它是可以买到的，但只有特斯拉认证的商店才能买到。”
特斯拉服务和维修信息门户允许车身商店注册并获得“免费账户访问”的认证。任何其他希望从特斯拉获取维修和诊断信息的人都必须付费订阅。100美元可以买到24小时的访问权。每年的订阅费是3000美元。
“他们非常聪明，”伯努瓦说。“希望这一次他们能给我们更多一点。”
特斯拉和其他汽车制造商一样，不希望第一个问题在马萨诸塞州通过。在投票之前，它向该州的特斯拉车主发送了一封电子邮件，敦促他们投反对票。
特斯拉长期以来一直将开源理念应用于我们的电动汽车专利知识产权。本着这一精神，我们向公众提供我们的服务、零部件和车身维修手册、接线图、维修公告、劳工代码和时间，以及其他用于对我们的车辆进行机械、电气和碰撞维修工作的信息。“特斯拉在电子邮件中说。
这在一定程度上是正确的，但特斯拉也多次使用软件，例如，将消费者锁在汽车的某些方面之外。特斯拉还与独立的维修公司进行了斗争，有时还拒绝对它们进行认证。
特斯拉说：“第一个问题远远超出了完成这项工作所必需的范围，它可能会危及车辆和数据安全。”“由两个全国性汽车商店游说团体推动的这些要求，将使车辆更容易受到网络攻击，并将使成功的攻击更具危害性。”这种对网络攻击的恐惧与汽车创新联盟(Alliance For Automotive Innovation)在该州的电视广告闪电战中使用的是一样的。但没起作用。
维修权对汽车业和特斯拉都很重要，特别是因为修理一辆特斯拉可能是一场代价高昂的噩梦。伯努瓦说：“该州只有两个修理点。”他说，特斯拉的销量已经达到顶峰，但“他们的维修基础设施就是不在那里。”
修理特斯拉最大的两个问题是等待时间和成本。业主往往要等上几周甚至几个月才能完成简单的工作。当车主真的把车修好时，费用往往很离奇。Benoit说，一些商店仅人工一项的收费就高达每小时200美元。伯努瓦说：“便宜一点的特斯拉3款售价3.5万美元。”他说：“这就像是日产Maxima的顶级产品。想象一下，为了诊断费，你每小时咳出200美元。这就是迈凯轮的价格。
Benoit知道还有更多的工作要做，他知道特斯拉将奋力拼搏每一步，以保住它的维修秘密，但他对未来充满希望。“这只是换个地方的问题，”他说。“这是人们的另一种选择。因为谁愿意花600美元买三个小时的刹车呢？
note_en:
A right-to-repair measure in Massachusetts  passed in a landslide victory last Tuesday. “Question 1,” as the measure was called on the ballot, will force car manufacturers to ensure independent repair stores have access to the same diagnostic tools as manufacturer run repair stores. That will change the automobile repair landscape in Massachusetts, especially for Tesla, a notoriously secretive company that has made it hard and expensive to service its electric vehicles.
    Rich Benoit, a  YouTuber and mechanic who  specializes in rebuilding busted Teslas, said that this right-to-repair bill is a game-changer, but that there’s still a lot of work to be done. Benoit has repeatedly run into issues sourcing parts and  accessing certain aspects of Tesla's software, as he detailed to Motherboard in a 2018 documentary.
   “For us, that means that starting the model year 2022, manufacturers have to give the same tools that they use at the dealerships available to a lot of third parties,” Benoit told Motherboard on the phone.
     Benoit is excited, but he knows that getting information and diagnostic tools is going to be a fight. He knows because Tesla has pushed back against these kinds of laws before. A similar law passed in 2012, one that said dealerships had to make service manuals and diagnostic information available to repair stores. Notably, Tesla also wasn't part of a memorandum of understanding signed by car manufacturers nationwide that effectively made the Massachusetts legislation a national law.
   “They got it around it by saying…they don’t have dealers,” Benoit said. “So technically, they didn’t have to do anything.”
   But Tesla did make some information available. “Out of the kindness of their heart, they made a portal so that you could log in and access their diagnostics,” Benoit said. “It’s available, but it’s only available to Tesla certified shops.”
    The Tesla Service and Repair Information Portal allows body shops to sign up and get certified for “complimentary account access.” Anyone else looking to get repair and diagnostic information out of Tesla has to pay for a subscription. $100 buys you 24 hours of access. An annual subscription costs $3,000.
   “They’re very clever,” Benoit said. “Hopefully, this time they give us a little bit more.”
   Tesla, like other automanufactuers, didn’t want Question 1 to pass in Massachusetts. Ahead of the vote, it sent an email to Tesla owners in the state urging them to vote no.
   “Tesla has long applied an open source philosophy to our patented intellectual property for electric vehicles. In this spirit, we provide public access to our service, parts, and body repair manuals, wiring diagrams, service bulletins, labor codes and times, and other information used to perform mechanical, electrical, and collision repair work on our vehicles,” Tesla said in the email.
   Some of this is true, but Tesla has also repeatedly, for example, used software to lock consumers out of certain aspects of their cars. Tesla has also  fought against independent repair companies, and has at times refused to certify them.
   “Question 1 goes well beyond what is necessary to perform this work, and it potentially jeopardizes vehicle and data security,” Tesla said. “The requirements, pushed by two national auto shop lobbying groups, would make vehicles more vulnerable to cyberattacks and would make successful attacks more harmful.” This fear of a cyber attack  is the same one the Alliance for Automotive Innovation used in its blitz of television ads in the state. It didn’t work.
    The right-to-repair is important for the automotive industry and Tesla specifically because repairing a Tesla can be a costly nightmare. “There’s only two repair locations in the state,” Benoit said. He said Tesla sales are through the roof but “their repair infrastructure just isn’t there.”
   The two biggest problems with repairing a Tesla are the wait time and cost. Owners often wait  weeks and even months for simple jobs to be finished. When an owner does get their car repaired, the costs are often outlandish. Benoit said that some shops charge upwards of $200 an hour for labor alone. “A cheaper model 3 Teslas costs $35,000,” Benoit said. “That’s like a top of the line Nissan Maxima. Imagine coughing up $200 an hour for a diagnostic fee. That’s McLaren prices.”
   Benoit knows there’s more work to do and he knows that Tesla will fight every step of the way to hold on to its repair secrets, but he’s hopeful about the future. “It’s just a matter of having another place,” he said. “Another option for people. Because who wants to pay $600 for a three hour brake job?”
title: 新通过的《维修权法》将从根本上改变特斯拉维修
title_en: Newly Passed Right-to-Repair Law Will Fundamentally Change Tesla Repair

note:
SpaceX的载人发射计划是根据商业机组计划开发的，NASA选择了两家私人公司建造宇航员发射系统，将宇航员从美国本土运送到国际空间站。2014年，NASA选择SpaceX和波音公司一起创建各自的系统。今年早些时候，SpaceX的龙舱和猎鹰9号火箭成功完成了最后一次Demo-2测试任务，成为第一个获得NASA实际载人飞行认证的火箭。Demo-2测试任务飞往国际空间站，机上有两名美国宇航员。
为了达到这一点，SpaceX公司必须成功完成一些里程碑，包括一次全自动的无人国际空间站会合任务，以及演示发射台中止和发射后中止紧急安全系统，以保护机组人员。在Demo-1任务中，虽然所有的实际发射、对接和着陆都是由SpaceX的全自动软件和导航处理的，但宇航员们也短暂地接管了手动控制，以展示如果需要，这个人工驾驶的后备设备将按预期运行。
到目前为止，Crew-1正在按预期进行，从佛罗里达进行了一次完美的起飞，并成功回收了用于发射龙的猎鹰9号火箭上使用的第一级助推器。“复原力”号机组人员也按计划在发射后10分钟后离开了猎鹰9号的第二阶段，在与国际空间站会合之前将在轨道上飞行27个小时，对接定于美国东部时间周一晚上11点左右(太平洋标准时间晚上8点)进行。一旦完全对接，宇航员们将下船并前往空间站开始他们的现役逗留，这一逗留将持续到明年6月。
这次任务的四名宇航员中有三名之前去过太空，但对于飞行员维克多·格洛弗来说，这是他第一次去太空。这四名宇航员将加入NASA的凯特·鲁宾斯(Kate Rubins)以及俄罗斯宇航员谢尔盖·雷日科夫(Sergey Ryzhikov)和谢尔盖·库德-斯维尔奇科夫(Sergey Kud-Sverchkov)的行列，使空间站的工作人员总数从通常的6人增加到7人(NASA表示，这将为宇航员腾出更多时间进行实验，而不是他们与空间站日常维护相关的任务)。
这是自2011年航天飞机计划结束以来，宇航员首次在NASA的常规运行任务中发射到太空。这标志着美国载人航天能力的正式回归，有望成为SpaceX和Dragon执行的许多载人飞行任务中的第一个-无论是NASA的飞行，还是商业客户组织的飞行。
note_en:
SpaceX’s human launch program was developed under the Commercial Crew program, which saw NASA select two private companies to build astronaut launch systems for carrying astronauts to the ISS from U.S. soil. SpaceX was chosen alongside  Boeing   by NASA in 2014 to create their respective systems, and SpaceX’s Dragon capsule and  Falcon   9 rocket became the first to achieve actual human flight certification from NASA earlier this year with the successful completion of its final, Demo-2 test mission, which flew to the ISS with two U.S. astronauts on board.
 To get to this point, SpaceX had to complete a number of milestones successfully, including a fully automated uncrewed ISS rendez-vous mission, and a demonstration of both a launch pad abort and post-launch abort emergency safety system for the protection of the crew. During the Demo-1 mission, while all actual launch, docking and landing was handled by SpaceX’s fully autonomous software and navigation, astronauts also took over manual control briefly to demonstrate that this human-piloted backup would operate as intended, if required.
  So far, Crew-1 is proceeding as expected, with a picture-perfect takeoff from Florida, and a successful recovery of the first-stage booster used on the Falcon 9 rocket used to launch Dragon. Crew Dragon ‘Resilience’ also departed from the second-stage of the Falcon 9 as planned at just after 10 minutes after liftoff, and there will be a 27 hour trip in orbit before the Dragon meets up with the ISS for its docking, which is scheduled to take place at around 11 PM EST (8 PM PST) on Monday night. Once fully docked, the astronauts will disembark and go over to the station to begin their active duty stay, which is set to last until next June.
  Three of the four astronauts on this mission have been to space previously, but for pilot Victor Glover, it’s his first time. These four will join NASA’s Kate Rubins, and Roscosmos cosmonauts Sergey Ryzhikov and Sergey Kud-Sverchkov on the station, bringing the total staff complement to seven (an increase from its usual six that NASA says will free up more time for the astronauts to perform experiments, as opposed to their tasks related to regular daily maintenance of the station).
 This is the first time that astronauts have launched to space during a regular operational NASA mission since the end of the Shuttle program in 2011. It marks an official return of U.S. human spaceflight capabilities, and should hopefully become the first in many human flight missions undertaken by SpaceX and Dragon – across both NASA flights, and those organized by commercial customers.
title: SpaceX和NASA成功将四名宇航员送入太空，执行首次执行的龙机组任务
title_en: SpaceX and NASA successfully launch four astronauts to space for first operational Dragon crew mission
